evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: The following guidance is based on the best available evidence. The full guideline gives details of the methods and the evidence used to develop the guidance. People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and safeguarding.
evidence_statements.source_title: Varicose veins: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults with varicose veins, lower-limb symptoms, skin changes, venous ulceration, or venous insufficiency context.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary care and vascular-service recognition and referral assessment.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Give people who present with varicose veins information that includes: An explanation of what varicose veins are. Possible causes of varicose veins. The likelihood of progression and possible complications, including deep vein thrombosis, skin changes, leg ulcers, bleeding and thrombophlebitis. Address any misconceptions the person may have about the risks of developing complications. Treatment options, including symptom relief, an overview of interventional treatments and the role of compression. Advice on: weight loss (for guidance on weight management, see the NICE guideline on overweight and obesity management ) light to moderate physical activity avoiding factors that are known to make their symptoms worse if possible when and where to seek further medical help.
evidence_statements.source_title: Varicose veins: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When discussing treatment for varicose veins at the vascular service tell the person: What treatment options are available. The expected benefits and risks of each treatment option. That new varicose veins may develop after treatment. That they may need more than 1 session of treatment. That the chance of recurrence after treatment for recurrent varicose veins is higher than for primary varicose veins. A vascular service is a team of healthcare professionals who have the skills to undertake a full clinical and duplex ultrasound assessment and provide a full range of treatment.

evidence_statements.population: Adults with varicose veins, lower-limb symptoms, skin changes, venous ulceration, or venous insufficiency context.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary care and vascular-service recognition and referral assessment.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people to a vascular service if they have any of the following. Symptomatic primary or symptomatic recurrent varicose veins. Symptomatic veins are veins found in association with troublesome lower limb symptoms (typically pain, aching, discomfort, swelling, heaviness and itching). Lower‑limb skin changes, such as pigmentation or eczema, thought to be caused by chronic venous insufficiency. Superficial vein thrombosis (characterised by the appearance of hard, painful veins) and suspected venous incompetence. A venous leg ulcer (a break in the skin below the knee that has not healed within 2 weeks). A healed venous leg ulcer.
evidence_statements.source_title: Varicose veins: diagnosis and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults with varicose veins, lower-limb symptoms, skin changes, venous ulceration, or venous insufficiency context.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary care and vascular-service recognition and referral assessment.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people with confirmed varicose veins and truncal reflux: Offer endothermal ablation (see NICE's interventional procedures guidance on radiofrequency ablation of varicose veins and endovenous laser treatment of the long saphenous vein ). If endothermal ablation is unsuitable, offer ultrasound‑guided foam sclerotherapy (see NICE's interventional procedures guidance on ultrasound-guided foam sclerotherapy for varicose veins ). If ultrasound‑guided foam sclerotherapy is unsuitable, offer surgery. If incompetent varicose tributaries are to be treated, consider treating them at the same time.
